bibliographic_data.title: Exploring Information Centrality for Intrusion Detection in Large Networks
bibliographic_data.abstract: Modern networked systems are constantly under threat from systemic attacks. There has been a massive upsurge in the number of devices connected to a network as well as the associated traffic volume. This has intensified the need to better understand all possible attack vectors during system design and implementation. Further, it has increased the need to mine large data sets, analyzing which has become a daunting task. It is critical to scale monitoring infrastructures to match this need, but a difficult goal for the small and medium organization. Hence, there is a need to propose novel approaches that address the big data problem in security. Information Centrality (IC) labels network nodes with better vantage points for detecting network-based anomalies as central nodes and uses them for detecting a category of attacks called systemic attacks. The main idea is that since these central nodes already see a lot of information flowing through the network, they are in a good position to detect anomalies before other nodes.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes using Information Centrality (IC) for identifying central nodes in large networks to accelerate anomaly detection and reduce data collection efforts.
core_content.key_methodology: The research introduces the use of Information Centrality, a centrality-based index, to identify key nodes in communication networks that can be used for detecting systemic attacks. The method involves modeling network simulations and analyzing behaviors under system-wide anomalies.
outcomes: Central nodes identified using IC can accelerate outlier detection when combined with suitable anomaly detection techniques.
A more efficient way to compute Information Centrality for large networks is proposed.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In the context of cyber security, this paper addresses the challenge of detecting systemic attacks in large networked systems. The authors propose using Information Centrality (IC) to identify central nodes that can provide a better vantage point for anomaly detection. By focusing on these key nodes, the method aims to reduce data collection efforts and improve the efficiency of intrusion detection systems. The research involves both theoretical analysis and experimental validation through simulations and network datasets.
